#  Conclusions

In conclusion, this work establishes that local heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) are the fundamental structural origin of the simultaneous enhancement in recoverable energy density and efficiency in antiferroelectric ceramics. Through a combination of phase-field simulations, atomic-scale HAADF-STEM imaging, and systematic electrical characterization, we demonstrate that controlled Cd doping in (Pb₀.₉₄₋ₓCdₓLa₀.₀₄)(Hf₀.₇Sn₀.₃)O₃ disrupts long-range AFE ordering, producing nanoscale mixtures of N=4 and N=5 modulation periods (Fig. 3c) and highly disordered dipole configurations. This disorder, visualized as diffuse contrast in dark-field images (Fig. 2e), suppresses hysteresis loss by enabling rapid domain reversion upon field removal and delays polarization saturation through local field redistribution. The resulting slimmer P–E loops and improved E_AF/E_FA characteristics yield a record W_rec of 14.6 J cm⁻³ with an efficiency of 81% in the Cd3 composition. However, critical questions remain: the precise control of HDS heterogeneity—balancing the degree of disorder against polarization saturation—is not yet achieved, and the dynamic evolution of HDSs under coupled thermal and electrical fields (Fig. 4) requires deeper mechanistic modeling. Future work should focus on quantitative descriptors of local disorder (e.g., correlation lengths, entropy metrics) and in situ characterization to guide rational design of next-generation antiferroelectric energy storage materials.
